mappings.rationale: Section C covers the nature of sociology including its background, methodology, status, and emergent trends.
Section B covers the nature of anthropology including its background, scope, and division between cultural and physical anthropology.
Section D covers the nature of economics including theories, scope, methods, and cognate disciplines like econometrics.
Section E covers the nature of political science including its history, scope, methods, and cognate disciplines.

mappings.vsiAuthor: John A. Matthews, David T. Herbert
mappings.rationale: Section F covers the nature of geography including its history, scope, and division between physical, human, and regional geography.
Section G covers the history and methods of psychology including its nature, scope, and special branches.
Section H covers the nature of linguistics including its history, nature, and scope.
Section C.5 lists social psychology as a cognate discipline of sociology, and G.3 mentions it as a special branch of psychology.
Section A.3.b specifically identifies the influence of Marxism as a major 20th-century development in the social sciences.
Section A.3.c identifies the influence of Freudian ideas as a major 20th-century development in the social sciences.
Section A.3.c covers the influence of Freudian psychoanalytic ideas on 20th-century social sciences.
Section C.5 lists demography as a cognate discipline of sociology.
Section C.5 lists criminology as a cognate discipline of sociology.
Section E.3 lists international relations as a cognate discipline of political science.
Section D.2 lists microeconomics as part of the scope and methods of the study of economics.
Behavioural economics is a development within economics (section D) incorporating psychological insights into economic theory.
Political philosophy connects to the nature of political science (section E) and the influence of political theories on social sciences.
Globalization is a cross-disciplinary social science topic touching economics, political science, and sociology as discussed in sections C-E.
Geopolitics connects political science (section E) with geography (section F), exemplifying cross-disciplinary approaches (A.3.d.i).
Sociolinguistics bridges sociology (section C) and linguistics (section H), illustrating the cross-disciplinary character of social sciences.
The study of languages relates to linguistics (section H) including its nature and scope.
The study of English relates to linguistics (section H) and the historical development of language study.
Multilingualism is a topic within linguistics (section H) connected to sociolinguistic and anthropological approaches.
Psycholinguistics bridges psychology (section G) and linguistics (section H), illustrating the interdisciplinary nature of these fields.
Section A.1.b identifies the heritage of the Enlightenment as a key origin of the social sciences through social reforms and revolution.
Intelligence research is a major branch of psychology discussed in section G on the history and methods of psychology.
Consciousness is a central topic in psychology (section G) and relates to physiological psychology (G.3).
The study of emotion spans psychology (section G) and anthropology (section B), both covered in this section.
Memory research is a core topic in psychology (section G), relevant to its nature, scope, and methods.
Dreaming research falls within psychology (section G) and connects to Freudian ideas that influenced social sciences (A.3.c).
Cognitive psychology including thinking and reasoning is a core area of psychology (section G).
Perception is a fundamental topic in psychology (section G), part of the nature and scope of psychological study.
The study of thought is a core concern of psychology (section G), connecting cognitive and philosophical approaches.
Child psychology is a special branch of psychology (section G) studying development and cognition.
Clinical psychology is a special branch of psychology (section G.3) focused on the assessment and treatment of mental disorders.
Forensic psychology is a special branch of psychology (section G.3) applied to legal and criminal justice contexts.
Cognitive neuroscience connects to physiological psychology (G.3) and represents the intersection of neuroscience and psychology.
The psychology of music is a special branch connecting psychology (section G) with the study of perception and cognition.
Creativity research spans psychology (section G) and social psychology (G.3), studying cognitive and social dimensions.
Adolescence is a topic in developmental psychology, a special branch of psychology (section G).
Happiness research spans psychology (section G) and is a growing area of empirical social science research.
Section A.2.b mentions social statistics as a developing discipline, and A.3.d.iii-iv covers quantitative methods and empiricism in social sciences.
Capitalism is a central concept in economics (section D) and relates to the influence of new economic theories on social sciences (A.2.a).
Feminism has been a major theoretical influence on sociology (section C) and the changing character of social science disciplines (A.3.d).
The study of ideology is central to political science (section E) and the theoretical frameworks of the social sciences.
Nationalism is a key topic in political science (section E) and sociology (section C), studied across multiple social science disciplines.
Democracy is a central concept in political science (section E), studied through its methods and cognate disciplines.
Populism is studied within political science (section E) as a political phenomenon analyzed through social science methods.
Physical anthropology, including human evolution, is part of the scope of anthropology discussed in section B.2.
Directly addresses the nature, scope, and methods of the social sciences that are the central subject of this section.
The study of slang is a topic within linguistics (section H), examining informal language use and change.
Translation studies are connected to linguistics (section H), involving the theoretical and practical study of language.

mappings.vsiAuthor: Emma Borg and Sarah A. Fisher
mappings.rationale: The study of meaning (semantics) is a core area of linguistics (section H).
The study of the self spans psychology (section G) and social psychology, examining identity, consciousness, and self-awareness.
Identity is studied across sociology (section C), anthropology (section B), and psychology (section G) as a cross-disciplinary topic.
Racism is studied within sociology (section C) and the broader social sciences as a social phenomenon with political dimensions.
International migration is studied across sociology, economics, and political science as a cross-disciplinary social science topic.
Tocqueville's work on democracy and society represents a foundational contribution to political science and sociology.
Malthus's work on population connects demography (C.5), economics (D), and the historical development of social science disciplines.
Adam Smith is foundational to the development of economics (section D) and the emergence of social science as a discipline.
Keynes is a pivotal figure in the development of economic theory (section D) and macroeconomics (D.2).
Synaesthesia is studied within psychology (section G) as a perceptual phenomenon with neurological and cognitive dimensions.
Imagination is studied within psychology (section G) as a cognitive faculty with implications for creativity and perception.
Learning is a fundamental topic in psychology (section G), studied through behavioral and cognitive approaches.
Negotiation draws on social psychology (G.3) and political science (E), studying interpersonal and intergroup conflict resolution.
The study of genius falls within psychology (section G), examining exceptional cognitive abilities and their origins.
Human geography is a social science discipline studying migration, inequality, climate, and spatial relationships, relevant to this section's coverage of the nature and scope of the social sciences.
